title: TaskFlow server gets slower throughout the day, requires daily restart
body:
Our self-hosted TaskFlow instance starts the day running fine but gets progressively slower. By late afternoon, page loads take 10+ seconds and the API times out. We've been restarting the server every evening for the past week. Memory usage on the server climbs from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ by end of day. We have about 200 active users.

I've attached a screenshot of our Grafana dashboard showing the memory climb. The pattern is very consistent day-to-day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report and the Grafana dashboard — that consistent memory climb is a really clear signal.

To help narrow down what's driving this, could you walk me through what a typical day of usage looks like for your team? For instance, are there particular times when usage spikes (like morning standup or end-of-day updates), and are your 200 users mostly doing the same kinds of things (viewing boards, creating tasks, running reports), or is there a mix of heavy and light workflows? I'm trying to understand whether the memory growth tracks with specific activity patterns or if it seems to climb steadily regardless of what people are doing.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Usage is pretty steady throughout the day honestly. We don't have big spikes — people trickle in starting around 8am and it's fairly constant until around 5pm. Most users are doing typical stuff: viewing their task boards, updating task statuses, adding comments, that sort of thing. Maybe 25 requests per user per hour on average based on our access logs. The memory growth is very linear and consistent, which is why I don't think it's tied to any specific workflow — it seems to just grow proportionally with overall request volume regardless of what people are actually doing.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — the linear growth proportional to request volume strongly suggests something is accumulating on a per-request basis rather than being tied to a specific feature.

You mentioned you've been doing daily restarts for the past week. Can you tell me more about what was happening before that? Was TaskFlow running fine for extended periods before this started, and if so, did anything change around the time it began — a version upgrade, a configuration change, adding a new integration or plugin, anything like that? I'm curious whether this is a recent shift in behavior or something that's been gradually getting worse over a longer period.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question — yes, this is definitely a recent thing. We upgraded from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3 about a week ago, and the memory issues started right after that. Before the upgrade, the server would run for weeks without any issues. We never needed to restart it. I actually checked the v2.3 changelog and one of the listed features was 'improved real-time notifications,' which stood out to me since the timing lines up perfectly. We didn't change any configuration or add any new integrations — it was just the version upgrade.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That timing with the v2.3 upgrade is really telling, and the 'improved real-time notifications' feature is a strong lead — linear memory growth per request is a classic pattern for something like event listeners or connections accumulating without being cleaned up.

I'm curious about a couple of related things: Are your users actively receiving real-time notifications (like popup alerts or live-updating task boards), and have you noticed whether the number of open WebSocket connections on the server grows over time the way the memory does? Also, does v2.3 offer any way to disable or configure the real-time notifications feature? If you could run for a day with that turned off, it would confirm whether that's the source of the leak — but even if you haven't tried that yet, knowing whether the option exists would help a developer narrow things down.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Yes, users do get real-time notifications — things like popups when someone assigns them a task or comments on something they're watching. Task boards also update live without needing to refresh the page. That was actually one of the selling points of the v2.3 upgrade.

I haven't specifically tracked WebSocket connection counts, but now that you mention it, I can check that. I do have access to server metrics through Grafana so I could add a panel for that. I'll take a look tomorrow during business hours when the pattern is actively happening.

As for disabling real-time notifications — I haven't looked into that honestly, but I can check the configuration docs. That's a great idea for confirming the source. I'd be willing to try running a day without it if the option exists, since daily restarts are already disruptive anyway.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Memory leak in v2.3 real-time notification system causes linear memory growth (~3.5GB/day), requiring daily restarts","problem":"After upgrading from TaskFlow v2.2 to v2.3, the server's memory usage climbs linearly from ~500MB at startup to 4GB+ over the course of a business day, causing progressively degraded performance (10+ second page loads, API timeouts) and requiring a daily restart. The issue affects all 200 active users by late afternoon.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The v2.3 'improved real-time notifications' feature is likely leaking resources on a per-request basis — most probably WebSocket connections, event listeners, or notification subscription objects that are created but never cleaned up when a user navigates away, closes a tab, or their session ends. The linear memory growth proportional to total request volume (not tied to any specific feature) suggests the leak occurs in a middleware or connection-handling layer common to all requests, rather than in a specific endpoint.","reproduction_steps":["Deploy TaskFlow v2.3 with default configuration on a self-hosted instance","Simulate steady usage (~5,000 requests/hour across multiple users) over several hours","Monitor server memory usage — expect linear growth of approximately 400-500MB per hour","Compare with v2.2 under identical load — expect stable memory"],"environment":"Self-hosted TaskFlow v2.3 (upgraded from v2.2 approximately one week ago), ~200 active users, steady usage pattern of ~25 requests/user/hour during business hours (8am-5pm), monitored via Grafana","severity":"high","impact":"All 200 users experience progressively degraded performance throughout the day, with the application becoming effectively unusable by late afternoon. Operations team must perform daily manual restarts, causing disruption.","recommended_fix":"Diff the v2.3 real-time notification implementation against v2.2. Investigate: (1) WebSocket connection lifecycle — are connections being closed when clients disconnect or navigate away? (2) Event listener/subscriber registration — are notification listeners being removed when no longer needed? (3) Any in-memory caches or data structures in the notification system that grow without bounds or eviction. Look for per-request allocations in middleware or connection-handling code that lack corresponding cleanup. A heap dump comparison between startup and after several hours of use would pinpoint the leaking objects.","proposed_test_case":"Run a load test simulating 200 concurrent users making 25 requests/hour each for 8 hours. Assert that server memory usage remains within a bounded range (e.g., does not exceed startup memory + 500MB) and that no WebSocket connections or event listeners accumulate beyond the number of active sessions. Run the same test with real-time notifications disabled to confirm isolation.","information_gaps":["Exact WebSocket connection count trend over time (reporter offered to check but hasn't yet — developer can verify directly)","Whether the v2.3 configuration allows disabling real-time notifications (useful for a quick mitigation, but not needed for root cause investigation)","Server-side language/runtime (e.g., Node.js, Java) — would help target specific memory profiling tools, but developer will know this from the codebase"]}
